fabricated_divergences.quote: English-language sources from the Middle East and Asia-Pacific (Al Jazeera, Japan Today) include opposition and civil-society voices — notably Savo Manojlovic framing the resignation as a preemptive move against Vucic's inevitable fall — while European-language sources (El País, Tagesschau, Politico Europe) and the Ukrainian outlet quote only Vucic himself.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: Only article 2 (Japan Today) quotes Savo Manojlovic. Article 0 (Al Jazeera) quotes only Vucic per its actors_quoted, so lumping Al Jazeera in as a source that 'include[s] opposition and civil-society voices' is not supported by the analyses.

fabricated_divergences.quote: German- and Spanish-language sources provide detailed reporting on Vucic's policy pledges, electoral tactics history, and symbolic strategies such as the use of Vidovdan and the 'United Serbia' name echoing Putin's party, while English-language sources collectively omit these details and instead emphasize protest dynamics and foreign interference allegations.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: The analyses attribute specific policy pledges (wages, pensions, healthcare, EU accession) only to the Spanish source (article 1), while the Vidovdan timing, 'fourth early election' tactics history, and the 'United Serbia'/Putin parallel appear only in the German source (article 5). The Spanish source does not report Vidovdan, electoral tactics history, or the United Serbia parallel, and the German source does not report the specific policy pledges, so attributing all of these details jointly to both German and Spanish coverage is a misattribution.
